[
  {
    "content": "You are an expert Linux kernel maintainer. Your goal is to perform a deep, rigorous review of a proposed kernel change to ensure safety, performance, and adherence to subsystem standards.

TOOL USAGE: When you need to gather information using tools, actively batch parallel or independent tool calls into a single response to minimize the number of conversation turns.

<global_review_guidelines>
The following documents contain the official technical patterns, architectural rules, and subsystem-specific guidelines that you MUST adhere to during your review. Use these as the absolute source of truth for identifying anti-patterns and violations.

@technical-patterns.md, @kconfig.md, @mm-folio.md, @mm-reclaim.md, @syscall.md

</global_review_guidelines>


Target Commit:
commit 34a5ab5c26571665fd31025bdb9817d2de6710f8
Author: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
Date:   Thu Mar 19 08:19:41 2026 +0000

    mm: introduce CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION and simplify CONFIG_MIGRATION
    
    CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTREMOVE, CONFIG_COMPACTION and CONFIG_CMA all select
    CONFIG_MIGRATION, because they require it to work (users).
    
    Only CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING and CONFIG_BALLOON_MIGRATION depend on
    CONFIG_MIGRATION. CONFIG_BALLOON_MIGRATION is not an actual user, but
    an implementation of migration support, so the dependency is correct
    (CONFIG_BALLOON_MIGRATION does not make any sense without
    CONFIG_MIGRATION).
    
    However, kconfig-language.rst  clearly states "In general use select only
    for non-visible symbols". So far CONFIG_MIGRATION is user-visible ...
    and the dependencies rather confusing.
    
    The whole reason why CONFIG_MIGRATION is user-visible is because of
    CONFIG_NUMA: some users might want CONFIG_NUMA but not page migration
    support.
    
    Let's clean all that up by introducing a dedicated CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION
    config option for that purpose only. Make CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING that so
    far depended on CONFIG_NUMA && CONFIG_MIGRATION to depend on
    CONFIG_MIGRATION instead. CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION will depend on
    CONFIG_NUMA && CONFIG_MMU.
    
    CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION is user-visible and will default to "y". We
    use that default so new configs will automatically enable it, just
    like it was the case with CONFIG_MIGRATION. The downside is that
    some configs that used to have CONFIG_MIGRATION=n might get it
    re-enabled by CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=y, which shouldn't be a problem.
    
    CONFIG_MIGRATION is now a non-visible config option. Any code that
    select CONFIG_MIGRATION (as before) must depend directly or indirectly
    on CONFIG_MMU.
    
    CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION is responsible for any NUMA migration code, which is
    mempolicy migration code, memory-tiering code, and move_pages() code in
    migrate.c. CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING uses its functionality.
    
    Note that this implies that with CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n, move_pages() will
    not be available even though CONFIG_MIGRATION=y, which is an expected
    change.
    
    In migrate.c, we can remove the CONFIG_NUMA check as both
    CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depend on it.
    
    With this change, CONFIG_MIGRATION is an internal config, all users of
    migration selects CONFIG_MIGRATION, and only CONFIG_BALLOON_MIGRATION
    depends on it.
    
    Signed-off-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

diff --git a/include/linux/memory-tiers.h b/include/linux/memory-tiers.h
index 96987d9d95a8..7999c58629ee 100644
--- a/include/linux/memory-tiers.h
+++ b/include/linux/memory-tiers.h
@@ -52,7 +52,7 @@ int mt_perf_to_adistance(struct access_coordinate *perf, int *adist);
 struct memory_dev_type *mt_find_alloc_memory_type(int adist,
 						  struct list_head *memory_types);
 void mt_put_memory_types(struct list_head *memory_types);
-#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION
 int next_demotion_node(int node, const nodemask_t *allowed_mask);
 void node_get_allowed_targets(pg_data_t *pgdat, nodemask_t *targets);
 bool node_is_toptier(int node);
diff --git a/init/Kconfig b/init/Kconfig
index 444ce811ea67..3648e401b78b 100644
--- a/init/Kconfig
+++ b/init/Kconfig
@@ -997,7 +997,7 @@ config NUMA_BALANCING
 	bool "Memory placement aware NUMA scheduler"
 	depends on ARCH_SUPPORTS_NUMA_BALANCING
 	depends on !ARCH_WANT_NUMA_VARIABLE_LOCALITY
-	depends on SMP && NUMA && MIGRATION && !PREEMPT_RT
+	depends on SMP && NUMA_MIGRATION && !PREEMPT_RT
 	help
 	  This option adds support for automatic NUMA aware memory/task placement.
 	  The mechanism is quite primitive and is based on migrating memory when
diff --git a/mm/Kconfig b/mm/Kconfig
index 9216d19db6ab..d8f99284ad3e 100644
--- a/mm/Kconfig
+++ b/mm/Kconfig
@@ -627,20 +627,20 @@ config PAGE_REPORTING
 	  those pages to another entity, such as a hypervisor, so that the
 	  memory can be freed within the host for other uses.
 
-#
-# support for page migration
-#
-config MIGRATION
-	bool "Page migration"
+config NUMA_MIGRATION
+	bool "NUMA page migration"
 	default y
-	depends on (NUMA || MEMORY_HOTREMOVE || COMPACTION || CMA) && MMU
-	help
-	  Allows the migration of the physical location of pages of processes
-	  while the virtual addresses are not changed. This is useful in
-	  two situations. The first is on NUMA systems to put pages nearer
-	  to the processors accessing. The second is when allocating huge
-	  pages as migration can relocate pages to satisfy a huge page
-	  allocation instead of reclaiming.
+	depends on NUMA && MMU
+	select MIGRATION
+	help
+	  Support the migration of pages to other NUMA nodes, available to
+	  user space through interfaces like migrate_pages(), move_pages(),
+	  and mbind(). Selecting this option also enables support for page
+	  demotion for memory tiering.
+
+config MIGRATION
+	bool
+	depends on MMU
 
 config DEVICE_MIGRATION
 	def_bool MIGRATION && ZONE_DEVICE
diff --git a/mm/memory-tiers.c b/mm/memory-tiers.c
index 986f809376eb..54851d8a195b 100644
--- a/mm/memory-tiers.c
+++ b/mm/memory-tiers.c
@@ -69,7 +69,7 @@ bool folio_use_access_time(struct folio *folio)
 }
 #endif
 
-#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION
 static int top_tier_adistance;
 /*
  * node_demotion[] examples:
@@ -129,7 +129,7 @@ static int top_tier_adistance;
  *
  */
 static struct demotion_nodes *node_demotion __read_mostly;
-#endif /* CONFIG_MIGRATION */
+#endif /* CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION */
 
 static BLOCKING_NOTIFIER_HEAD(mt_adistance_algorithms);
 
@@ -273,7 +273,7 @@ static struct memory_tier *__node_get_memory_tier(int node)
 				     lockdep_is_held(&memory_tier_lock));
 }
 
-#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION
 bool node_is_toptier(int node)
 {
 	bool toptier;
@@ -519,7 +519,7 @@ static void establish_demotion_targets(void)
 
 #else
 static inline void establish_demotion_targets(void) {}
-#endif /* CONFIG_MIGRATION */
+#endif /* CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION */
 
 static inline void __init_node_memory_type(int node, struct memory_dev_type *memtype)
 {
@@ -911,7 +911,7 @@ static int __init memory_tier_init(void)
 	if (ret)
 		panic("%s() failed to register memory tier subsystem\n", __func__);
 
-#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION
 	node_demotion = kzalloc_objs(struct demotion_nodes, nr_node_ids);
 	WARN_ON(!node_demotion);
 #endif
@@ -938,7 +938,7 @@ subsys_initcall(memory_tier_init);
 
 bool numa_demotion_enabled = false;
 
-#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION
 #ifdef CONFIG_SYSFS
 static ssize_t demotion_enabled_show(struct kobject *kobj,
 				     struct kobj_attribute *attr, char *buf)
diff --git a/mm/mempolicy.c b/mm/mempolicy.c
index e5528c35bbb8..fd08771e2057 100644
--- a/mm/mempolicy.c
+++ b/mm/mempolicy.c
@@ -1239,7 +1239,7 @@ static long do_get_mempolicy(int *policy, nodemask_t *nmask,
 	return err;

-#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION

 				unsigned long flags)
 {
diff --git a/mm/migrate.c b/mm/migrate.c
index fdbb20163f66..05cb408846f2 100644
--- a/mm/migrate.c
+++ b/mm/migrate.c
@@ -2224,8 +2224,7 @@ struct folio *alloc_migration_target(struct folio *src, unsigned long private)
 	return __folio_alloc(gfp_mask, order, nid, mtc->nmask);
 }
 
-#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA
-
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION
 static int store_status(int __user *status, int start, int value, int nr)
 {
 	while (nr-- > 0) {
@@ -2624,6 +2623,7 @@ SYSCALL_DEFINE6(move_pages, pid_t, pid, unsigned long, nr_pages,
 {
 	return kernel_move_pages(pid, nr_pages, pages, nodes, status, flags);

+#endif /* CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION */
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING

@@ -2766,4 +2766,3 @@ int migrate_misplaced_folio(struct folio *folio, int node)
 	return nr_remaining ? -EAGAIN : 0;

-#endif /* CONFIG_NUMA */

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Conditional Compilation of `BALLOON_MIGRATION` Dependencies**

Alright, let's break this down. My understanding is that `BALLOON_MIGRATION` relies on `MIGRATION`. So, to figure out how this is all working, I'll need to dig into the code and see how these pieces fit together, specifically in `mm/migrate.c`.

Okay, I'm looking at `migrate.c`, and it looks like the patch I'm examining primarily affects `move_pages` and its associated system calls. Now, the interesting bit here is that these changes are all behind an `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` block. Hmm... that's a key detail to watch for. If I configure the kernel with `CONFIG_NUMA=y`, but, crucially, `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, that means `sys_move_pages` will *not* be compiled. It will be completely excluded.

Okay, so I'm digging into the NUMA migration code and something's bugging me. In `mm/mempolicy.c`, at line 1467, `do_migrate_pages` will return `-ENOSYS` if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled. But wait, in the header file `include/linux/mempolicy.h`, `do_migrate_pages` is *already* stubbed to return 0 if `CONFIG_NUMA` itself isn't enabled.

The situation now is that `CONFIG_NUMA` is enabled, but `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled.  So, `kernel_migrate_pages` gets called, it parses the node information, finds the target task, does some security checks, and *then* it calls `do_migrate_pages`, which promptly returns `-ENOSYS`.

That's where I get a little concerned.  It feels a bit wrong that a syscall path, which ultimately returns "not supported" (-ENOSYS) after having a few arguments parsed.  Typically, if a syscall is completely unsupported at compile time, we would have something that directly routes to `sys_ni_syscall` or just immediately returns `-ENOSYS`.  But here, even though the feature is disabled, `sys_migrate_pages` is still compiled, and the argument parsing *still happens* before the `-ENOSYS` is returned.  Is this the intended behavior? It's not the end of the world but, it feels like it could be cleaner.

Let me circle back to `move_pages` and see what's going on there. If `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled, `sys_move_pages` in `mm/migrate.c` is *not* compiled because it's enclosed in an `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` block in `mm/migrate.c`. Good, that makes sense. Wait... I can't find a fallback definition for `sys_move_pages` if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, is this right?

Ah, right! I remember, there's a `COND_SYSCALL(move_pages)` in `kernel/sys_ni.c`. So if `sys_move_pages` is undefined in `mm/migrate.c`, the one in `sys_ni.c` takes over and returns `-ENOSYS`. Okay, that's consistent.

But, circling back, `sys_migrate_pages` *is* in `mm/mempolicy.c`, which gets compiled as long as `CONFIG_NUMA` is enabled. And, critically, `kernel_migrate_pages` does *not* have an `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` block around it. So even with `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` disabled, the kernel still does some up front argument parsing for `migrate_pages` before the `-ENOSYS` gets returned! This warrants another look.

Okay, so I'm digging into this NUMA stuff, and something feels off. If `CONFIG_NUMA` is enabled, but `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled, the `kernel_migrate_pages` function *still* gets executed. It fetches user memory, does some process verification, and then finally calls `do_migrate_pages`, which then returns `-ENOSYS`.

The odd thing is, is this standard behavior for syscalls? Usually, if a feature is disabled at config time, you'd expect the syscall to just return `-ENOSYS` immediately, wouldn't you? It seems like an unnecessary overhead to parse arguments first when you know the functionality is disabled. But maybe it's not a big deal.

However, a concern comes to mind. What happens if someone calls `migrate_pages` with a bad, invalid pointer? They'll get `-EFAULT` instead of `-ENOSYS`. It's a subtle difference from a user perspective, but it *is* a difference. Is this a bug? It’s probably just a quirk.

Hold on, there's another point. What about `mbind`? I know `mbind` can also do page migration, especially when using `MPOL_MF_MOVE`. I need to look at `kernel_mbind` in `mm/mempolicy.c` to see how it handles this scenario. Gotta investigate that now.

Okay, let me break this down. I'm digging into how `mbind` behaves, specifically when the `MPOL_MF_MOVE` flag is used, and how it interacts with different kernel configuration options. I'm focusing on the case where `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is off, but `CONFIG_COMPACTION` might still be enabled.

First, I see that within `do_mbind`, the call to `queue_pages_range` is key. My initial thought was whether `MPOL_MF_MOVE` is even supported if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled. The code shows that `migrate_folio_add` is defined under `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`, meaning it returns `false` if the config option is disabled.

Then, I trace the execution flow. With `MPOL_MF_MOVE` set and `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, `migrate_folio_add` consistently fails. This causes `queue_folios_pte_range` to add entries to `qp->nr_failed`. Then, in `do_mbind`, these are eventually collected in a `pagelist`. But here's the catch: the code also has a check for `(flags & (MPOL_MF_MOVE | MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL))` which includes a call to `lru_cache_disable()`.

The critical observation is that if `migrate_folio_add` always returns `false`, `pagelist` remains empty. Then, the code calls `migrate_pages` but with an empty `pagelist`! I need to confirm whether `queue_pages_range` returns an error if `migrate_folio_add` fails, as an empty list seems unexpected. `strictly_unmovable` doesn't seem to be a factor because it is only considered if the `STRICT` bit is set, which isn't the case. If `MPOL_MF_MOVE` is requested, it will always be false.

The next point is about `migrate_pages`. I had to confirm if `migrate.c` gets compiled even when `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled. I verified that, yes, it does, because while `CONFIG_NUMA=y` doesn't imply `CONFIG_MIGRATION=n`, having `CONFIG_COMPACTION=y` implies `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y`. But then the empty list means `migrate_pages` will do nothing, because it has nothing to migrate!

If `migrate_folio_add` returns `false`, then `qp->nr_failed` increases. Therefore, if `nr_failed > 0` and the `STRICT` flag is also set, an error is returned. That might be a user error in that they are using `MPOL_MF_STRICT`, because `MPOL_MF_VALID` is defined as `STRICT | MOVE | MOVE_ALL`. The main thing is that, with `MPOL_MF_MOVE` enabled but with `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, `mbind` quietly succeeds without actually moving pages.

However, the change decouples these, and now it's possible to have `CONFIG_COMPACTION=y`, `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y` but `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`. Initially, I was concerned about the change from `#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION` to `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` for `migrate_folio_add` in `mm/mempolicy.c`. However, after considering all the dependencies, I'm starting to see the logic.

The worry was, would the conditional compilation of `sys_migrate_pages` now lead to a different behavior with respect to the `-ENOSYS` error? But no, it appears not. Before the change, if migration was disabled, `do_migrate_pages` would return `-ENOSYS`, which is the same as the new conditional compilation.

The biggest shift is related to `sys_move_pages`. Previously, it was tied to `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA` in `mm/migrate.c`. If `CONFIG_MIGRATION=n`, `mm/migrate.c` was never compiled in the first place, thus the call was resolved by `COND_SYSCALL` in `kernel/sys_ni.c` and immediately returned `-ENOSYS`. Now, with `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`, even if `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y` but `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, `sys_move_pages` isn't compiled, and the same `-ENOSYS` behavior holds. So, the essential behavior is unchanged.

The more significant structural change involves `mm/migrate.c` and the removal of the enclosing `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA` block, especially concerning `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING`. The patch now makes it so that `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` lives outside the previous `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA`. But `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` depends on `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`, which means that `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` can only be `y` if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is also `y`, and thus, so is `CONFIG_NUMA=y`. Thus, this refactoring doesn't change the build dependency, or the final outcome. It makes things clearer.

The major question, then, is about UAPI compatibility. Did we break anything? But it appears we're fine. The original dependencies, and the patch's implementation, all boil down to "if `CONFIG_NUMA=y`, then the NUMA page migration UAPIs are available." Finally, I checked `kernel_move_pages`. The patch's change appears to be localized, and `alloc_migration_target` appears unaffected. All the functions used internally by `kernel_move_pages` are also conditionally compiled based on `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`, which should leave all of the previous logic intact.     "thought": "**Analyzing the Impact of Disabling `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` on Page Node Queries**

Okay, let me think this through. My initial understanding was that `sys_move_pages` with `nodes == NULL` was for page migration, but it's really just a way to execute `do_pages_stat`, which *only* returns NUMA node information, not move the pages themselves. The architecture should be updated.

Now, if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, `sys_move_pages` and therefore `do_pages_stat` is disabled. That's fine, I understand the goal. The `move_pages` system call has two modes, moving or returning the page status.

However, a potential problem emerges: If I have `CONFIG_NUMA=y` and `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, `sys_move_pages` is disabled. This means userspace can't use `move_pages` to *query* the NUMA node of a page! Is that the expected behavior? The commit message seems to confirm this is the plan.

I see that the commit message says this is an expected change. But is that right?

Specifically, `move_pages(pid, nr, pages, NULL, status, 0)` is used to query where pages are in NUMA nodes. It doesn't actually require migration. By putting the ENTIRE `sys_move_pages` behind `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`, querying page nodes also becomes unavailable! That has to be revisited.

Is there another way to get this information? `get_mempolicy(..., MPOL_F_ADDR)` is insufficient, as `move_pages` is designed to query the actual node of a set of pages. This is a crucial part of tools like `numastat`, `numactl`, and HPC applications to verify memory placement.

If a user has `CONFIG_NUMA=y` but *deliberately* sets `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, they still have a NUMA system and might reasonably want to know their page placement, but this patch blocks them from doing so! Is that an acceptable tradeoff?

The author notes that this is the desired behavior, that `move_pages()` will become unavailable if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`.

But, before this patch, if `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y` (e.g., pulled in by `COMPACTION`), `sys_move_pages` WAS available, EVEN if the user didn't care about NUMA page migration!

So, to summarize: Before this change, if `CONFIG_NUMA=y` and `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y` (because `COMPACTION=y`), `sys_move_pages` was available, and could provide the functionality of both `do_pages_stat` and `do_pages_move`.

Now, if a user has `CONFIG_NUMA=y`, `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, and `CONFIG_COMPACTION=y`: before the patch, `sys_move_pages` was available; after the patch, it's NOT available!

`CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` defaults to `y`, so the common case is unaffected. However, the author explicitly intended for `move_pages()` to become unavailable if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`.

Let me examine the code in `mm/migrate.c` to see if there is another way to achieve the same result.

Okay, so I'm digging into this code, and it seems like `do_migrate_pages` is correctly returning `-ENOSYS` in `mm/mempolicy.c` when `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is off, which is as expected. We know `sys_migrate_pages` is only compiled out when `CONFIG_NUMA=n`.  If `CONFIG_NUMA` is enabled but `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled, the system still goes through the memory allocation and checks *before* returning the error, but that's alright, that’s similar to how `sys_mbind` behaves with `STRICT` and no migration support.

But hang on… the real issue seems to be with how `sys_move_pages` and `do_pages_stat` are handled.  Let me think through the implications of these changes.

First, I see that `CONFIG_MIGRATION` is no longer a direct user prompt (it's a `bool` now, automatically selected by various other configs). `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` handles `move_pages`, `migrate_pages`, and `mbind(MPOL_MF_MOVE)`. And it appears that if `move_pages` is called with `nodes == NULL`, it executes `do_pages_stat`, which reports the current node of a page.

Now, this is where it gets interesting.  By removing `do_pages_stat` when `CONFIG_NUMA` is enabled *but* `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled, are we breaking any UAPI? Yes! `do_pages_stat` has nothing to do with migration itself. It maps a virtual address to a physical node. Disabling it cripples user-space monitoring tools (like `numastat`, page queries, HPC placement verification), even when migration *isn't* the primary use case. By putting all of `sys_move_pages` behind `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`, we're wrongly gating this observability feature.

Okay, let's trace `kernel_move_pages` again, because it all seems so oddly familiar. Before this patch, both `kernel_move_pages` and `sys_move_pages` were inside the `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA`. So, the `mm/migrate.c` file was compiled only if `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y`. With `CONFIG_NUMA=y` and `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y`, `sys_move_pages` was available. However, if `CONFIG_NUMA=y` and `CONFIG_MIGRATION=n`, `mm/migrate.c` was not compiled, meaning `sys_move_pages` was unavailable.

So here's the kicker: Before this patch, `do_pages_stat` was *already* gated behind `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y`! Because, `do_pages_stat` was implemented in `mm/migrate.c`, which was only compiled when `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y`. So users with `CONFIG_NUMA=y` and `CONFIG_MIGRATION=n` were already SOL. But `CONFIG_MIGRATION` was almost always `y`, because it was user-visible, defaulted to `y`, and selected by `COMPACTION` (which defaults to `y`).

Now, `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is what's user-visible, and it defaults to `y`, but it depends on `NUMA && MMU`. A user who explicitly disables `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` will lose `do_pages_stat`. But, before the patch, a user explicitly disabling `CONFIG_MIGRATION` (and without things like `COMPACTION`) *also* lost `do_pages_stat`.

So, in essence, gating `sys_move_pages` behind `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` *exactly* mirrors the prior gating.  Except now it's just tied to `NUMA_MIGRATION` instead of `MIGRATION`, which used to be the only path to NUMA migration.  So, no actual regression. Maybe we can decouple `do_pages_stat` from the migration feature itself? But it *is* part of the `sys_move_pages` system call and is conceptually associated with page movement, even if `nodes == NULL` is used to just get stats.

The search confirmed that the definition for `move_pages` is correctly within the `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` block in `mm/migrate.c`. Good, expected. And, as the check in `kernel/sys_ni.c` correctly handles the case where it's not compiled, falling back to `-ENOSYS`, as I'd expect.

Now, let's see about this `mm/mempolicy.c` diff. Aha, it seems like `migrate_folio_add` is the key. Originally, this function was guarded by `CONFIG_MIGRATION`, but now it's under `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`. Hmm, and after a moment's consideration, yes, `migrate_folio_add` *is* primarily related to NUMA migration. It's used by the `mbind` and related syscalls, which are, of course, NUMA specific.

The interesting question is, what happens if `CONFIG_MIGRATION` is enabled, but `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled? I'm already thinking that `mm/mempolicy.c` *is* compiled when `CONFIG_NUMA` is enabled. It's almost the central configuration element for enabling NUMA capabilities. Before the patch, `mm/mempolicy.c` used `CONFIG_MIGRATION`, but now it exclusively uses `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`.

Let's not jump to conclusions. I need to get a complete view. Is there anything else in `mm/mempolicy.c` inside this block, and what exactly is the effect on the rest of `mm/mempolicy.c`? I have to analyze the entire block.

Okay, let's break this down. First, I wanted to confirm if the `CONFIG_MIGRATION` usage has been replaced with `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` in `mm/mempolicy.c`. Seems like that's correct, `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is the successor. Looking into `mm/Kconfig`, `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` depends on `NUMA` and `MMU` and selects `MIGRATION`. Now, I need to check `move_pages_huge_pmd` in `include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h` and `mm/huge_memory.c`, to ensure there's no collision with the `move_pages` syscall that userfaultfd also uses. No worries, the syscall is called `sys_move_pages`, all clear.

Now, I'm focusing back on `mm/mempolicy.c` and specifically at `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol`. This function, which allocates a target folio for migration, is guarded by `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`. If `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is *not* defined, this function returns `NULL`. The interesting part is in `do_mbind` where the following check exists: `if (!err && !list_empty(&pagelist))`. It calls `migrate_pages` only if `pagelist` is not empty.  So, I had to ensure that `pagelist` is indeed empty when `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is false.

I dove into `queue_pages_pte_range` to see how `pagelist` is populated. It calls `migrate_folio_add`. If `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is false, `migrate_folio_add` is a no-op, always returning `false`. That means the folio will not be added to `pagelist` and `qp->nr_failed` is incremented. So, `pagelist` *should* always be empty when `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is false.

Wait, but what about hugetlb pages? I need to consider `queue_folios_hugetlb`.  I saw `folio_isolate_hugetlb(folio, qp->pagelist)` is called.  This function is used for hugetlb folios and it does *not* go through `migrate_folio_add`! That's a crucial difference!  I need to dig into `queue_folios_hugetlb` again, and make sure that it deals with the situation correctly.

Okay, so I'm looking at how hugetlb folios are handled when `NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled. I see that `queue_folios_hugetlb` adds the folio to a `pagelist` using `folio_isolate_hugetlb`.  The key here is that this happens regardless of NUMA migration.  

If `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is false, this `pagelist` then gets passed to `migrate_pages` within `do_mbind`. My initial thought is that since `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol` will return `NULL` in this scenario, and `migrate_pages` is called, the allocation of new pages will fail within `migrate_pages` which should then return an error or `nr_failed`. But... wait! This seems wrong. Why are we isolating the folio in the first place? If we aren't migrating, shouldn't we be avoiding this entirely?

Digging deeper, I see that the `queue_folios_hugetlb` function itself isn't gated by either `CONFIG_MIGRATION` or `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`. This calls `folio_isolate_hugetlb`.

However, `migrate_pages` *is* guarded by `CONFIG_MIGRATION`, at least conceptually in `include/linux/migrate.h`. Before this patch, if `CONFIG_MIGRATION` was false, `migrate_pages` would be a static inline function returning `-ENOSYS` and then calls `putback_movable_pages`.

But here's the kicker: If `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is false, but `CONFIG_MIGRATION` is *true*, `migrate_pages` *is* the real function. So now, `migrate_pages` will still be called even though `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol` returns NULL which then means `get_new_folio` will return NULL, and `migrate_pages` will iterate through the pages.

Now I need to understand what happens *inside* `migrate_pages` when `get_new_folio` returns NULL. I need to examine `migrate_pages_batch` or `unmap_and_move` within `mm/migrate.c` to see how the failure is handled. I need to figure out if this path will lead to a resource leak, or potentially other unexpected behavior.

Okay, let's break this down systematically. I'm focusing on the implications of `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol` returning `NULL`, particularly in relation to hugetlb pages and NUMA settings.

Initially, I'm tracing the flow in `unmap_and_move_huge_page`. If `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol` fails with `-ENOMEM`, `unmap_and_move_huge_page` will return `-ENOMEM`. This cascades up to `migrate_hugetlbs`, which, in turn, can also return `-ENOMEM`. Ultimately, we end up in `do_mbind`, which calls `migrate_pages`. Now, the important question is: what is the user-space behavior in this scenario?  If `migrate_pages` returns an error, does `mbind` return an error?

I see that `mbind` calls `migrate_pages` and then checks `nr_failed`. The return from `migrate_pages` is *bitwise ORed* into `nr_failed`. Then there is a conditional check on `err`. Here's where it gets interesting: the return code from `mbind` itself is *not* the error code from `migrate_pages`! It only uses it to set the final error code if `MPOL_MF_STRICT` is true, otherwise, it returns 0. If `migrate_pages` encounters a `-ENOMEM` (or any error), the folios that could not be migrated will be put back on the list (pagelist). So this ensures no memory leaks.

Previously, with `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` disabled, `migrate_pages` would return `-ENOSYS`. Now, even if `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol` fails, it returns a code (potentially `-ENOMEM`), but `mbind` still returns 0 (unless `STRICT` is requested, in which case it returns `-EIO`). This is *consistent* with the old behavior because, even though the migration *failed* in the background, the user isn't directly notified. This is not strictly a *failure* because it only didn't move the pages.

Now, let's consider the broader implications. It looks like `migrate_pages_batch` handles the negative return code from `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol` correctly for non-hugetlb pages as well.

Moving on to `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` and its relationship with other configurations: I see that `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` selects `MIGRATION` and that `CONFIG_CMA` (Contiguous Memory Allocator) also selects `MIGRATION`. It looks like `MIGRATION` is now a hidden option. Importantly, if NUMA is disabled, `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is also disabled. I also confirm that the NUMA-specific migration APIs (like `move_pages` and `alloc_migration_target`) are properly guarded by `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`.

Now, what about `do_migrate_pages`?  If `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled, it returns `-ENOSYS`. The key question is: what calls `do_migrate_pages`? I find that `cpuset` uses it. Specifically, `cpuset_migrate_mm_workfn` calls `do_migrate_pages`. This means that if NUMA migration is disabled, calls from cpuset would just "do nothing" - which is consistent with the pre-patch behavior.

Finally, I notice that `alloc_migration_target()` is defined in `mm/migrate.c`, *but it is not guarded by `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`*. This is worth double-checking. Now, `alloc_migration_target` actually returns a `struct migration_target_control`, which is used in `do_move_pages_to_node`, `migrate_to_node`, and `alloc_migration_target` - the first two are inside `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`. Then I need to check where `alloc_migration_target()` itself is used to see if the lack of `#ifdef` is problematic. Checking `mm/memory_hotplug.c` and other callers of `alloc_migration_target()` would be my next step, but so far, things seem consistent, although a closer look at `mm/memory_hotplug.c` may be needed, to ensure that the logic within `memory_hotplug` is correct.

Is this actually the intended behavior? Let's see... Ah, yes! The commit message explicitly states that with `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION=n`, `move_pages()` *will not* be available, even if `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y`. It confirms this as an expected change. Alright, that makes sense.

Now, let's drill down into `CONFIG_MIGRATION` and its dependencies. Let me see... Before this patch, `CONFIG_MIGRATION` depended on `(NUMA || MEMORY_HOTREMOVE || COMPACTION || CMA) && MMU`. It was also set as a default, which was "y".

Now, it's defined like this:
```kconfig
config NUMA_MIGRATION

	default y
	depends on NUMA && MMU
	select MIGRATION

config MIGRATION
	bool
	depends on MMU
```

They relied on the fact that `CONFIG_MIGRATION` would automatically be enabled if they were enabled (since it was "default y" and its dependencies would be satisfied). But is that *still* true? Hopefully we can\n# add proper SWAP support to them, in which case this can be remove.\n#\nconfig ARCH_NO_SWAP\n\tbool\n\nmenuconfig SWAP\n\tbool \"Support for paging of anonymous memory (swap)\"\n\tdepends on MMU && BLOCK && !ARCH_NO_SWAP\n\tdefault y\n\thelp\n\t  This option allows you to choose whether you want to have support\n\t  for so called swap devices or swap files in your kernel that are\n\t  used to provide more virtual memory than the actual RAM present\n\t  in your computer.  If unsure say Y.\n\nconfig ZSWAP\n\tbool \"Compressed cache for swap pages\"\n\tdepends on SWAP\n\tselect CRYPTO\n\tselect ZSMALLOC\n\thelp\n\t  A lightweight compressed cache for swap pages.  It takes\n\t  pages that are in the process of being swapped out and attempts to\n\t  compress them into a dynamically allocated RAM-based memory pool.\n\t  This can result in a significant I/O reduction on swap device and,\n\t  in the case where decompressing from RAM is faster than swap device\n\t  reads, can also improve workload performance.\n\nconfig ZSWAP_DEFAULT_ON\n\tbool \"Enable the compressed cache for swap pages by default\"\n\tdepends on ZSWAP\n\thelp\n\t  If selected, the compressed cache for swap pages will be enabled\n\t  at boot, otherwise it will be disabled.\n\n\t  The selection made here can be overridden by using the kernel\n\t  command line 'zswap.enabled=' option.\n\nconfig ZSWAP_SHRINKER_DEFAULT_ON\n\tbool \"Shrink the zswap pool on memory pressure\"\n\tdepends on ZSWAP\n\tdefault n\n\thelp\n\t  If selected, the zswap shrinker will be enabled, and the pages\n\t  stored in the zswap pool will become available for reclaim (i.e\n\t  written back to the backing swap device) on memory pressure.\n\n\t  This means that zswap writeback could happen even if the pool is\n\t  not yet full, or the cgroup zswap limit has not been reached,\n\t  reducing the chance that cold pages will reside in the zswap pool\n\t  and consume memory indefinitely.\n\nchoice\n\tprompt \"Default compressor\"\n\tdepends on ZSWAP\n\tdefault ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_LZO\n\thelp\n\t  Selects the default compression algorithm for the compressed cache\n\t  for swap pages.\n\n\t  For an overview what kind of performance can be expected from\n\t  a particular compression algorithm please refer to the benchmarks\n\t  available at the following LWN page:\n\t  https://lwn.net/Articles/751795/\n\n\t  If in doubt, select 'LZO'.\n\n\t  The selection made here can be overridden by using the kernel\n\t  command line 'zswap.compressor=' option.\n\nconfig ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_DEFLATE\n\tbool \"Deflate\"\n\tselect ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_LZ4HC\n\tbool \"LZ4HC\"\n\tselect CRYPTO_LZ4HC\n\thelp\n\t  Use the LZ4HC algorithm as the default compression algorithm.\n\nconfig ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_ZSTD\n\tbool \"zstd\"\n\tselect CRYPTO_ZSTD\n\thelp\n\t  Use the zstd algorithm as the default compression algorithm.\nendchoice\n\nconfig ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT\n       string\n       depends on ZSWAP\n       default \"deflate\" if ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_DEFLATE\n       default \"lzo\" if ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_LZO\n       default \"842\" if ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_842\n       default \"lz4\" if ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_LZ4\n       default \"lz4hc\" if ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_LZ4HC\n       default \"zstd\" if ZSWAP_COMPRESSOR_DEFAULT_ZSTD\n       default \"\"\n\nconfig \"Export zsmalloc statistics\"\n\tselect DEBUG_FS\n\thelp\n\t  This option enables code in the zsmalloc to collect various\n\t  statistics about what's happening in zsmalloc and exports that\n\t  information to userspace via debugfs.\n\t  If unsure, say N.\n\nconfig ZSMALLOC_CHAIN_SIZE\n\tint \"Maximum number of physical pages per-zspage\"\n\tdefault 8\n\trange 4 16\n\thelp\n\t  This option sets the upper limit on the number of physical pages\n\t  that a zmalloc page (zspage) can consist of. The optimal zspage\n\t  chain size is calculated for each size class during the\n\t  initialization of the pool.\n\n\t  Changing this option can alter the characteristics of size classes,\n\t  such as the number of pages per zspage and the number of objects\n\t  per zspage. This can also result in different configurations of\n\t  the pool, as zsmalloc merges size classes with similar\n\t  characteristics.\n\n\t  For more information, see zsmalloc documentation.\n\nendmenu\n\nendif\n\nmenu \"Slab allocator options\"\n\nconfig SLUB\n\tdef_bool y\n\tselect IRQ_WORK\n\nconfig KVFREE_RCU_BATCHED\n\tdef_bool y\n\tdepends on !SLUB_TINY && !TINY_RCU\n\nconfig SLUB_TINY\n\tbool \"Configure for minimal memory footprint\"\n\tdepends on EXPERT && !COMPILE_TEST\n\tselect SLAB_MERGE_DEFAULT\n\thelp\n\t   Configures the slab allocator in a way to achieve minimal memory\n\t   footprint, sacrificing scalability, debugging and other features.\n\t   This is intended only for the smallest system that had used the\n\t   SLOB allocator and is not recommended for systems with more than\n\t   16MB RAM.\n\n\t   If unsure, say N.\n\nconfig SLAB_MERGE_DEFAULT\n\tbool \"Allow slab caches to be merged\"\n\tdefault y\n\thelp\n\t  For reduced kernel memory fragmentation, slab caches can be\n\t  merged when they share the same size and other characteristics.\n\t  This carries a risk of kernel heap overflows being able to\n\t  overwrite objects from merged caches (and more easily control\n\t  cache layout), which makes such heap attacks easier to exploit\n\t  by attackers. By keeping caches unmerged, these kinds of exploits\n\t  can usually only damage objects in the same cache. To disable\n\t  merging at runtime, \"slab_nomerge\" can be passed on the kernel\n\t  command line.\n\nconfig SLAB_FREELIST_RANDOM\n\tbool \"Randomize slab freelist\"\n\tdepends on !SLUB_TINY\n\thelp\n\t  Randomizes the freelist order used on creating new pages. This\n\t  security feature reduces the predictability of the kernel slab\n\t  allocator against heap overflows.\n\nconfig SLAB_FREELIST_HARDENED\n\tbool \"Harden slab freelist metadata\"\n\tdepends on !SLUB_TINY\n\thelp\n\t  Many kernel heap attacks try to target slab cache metadata and\n\t  other infrastructure. This options makes minor performance\n\t  sacrifices to harden the kernel slab allocator against common\n\t  freelist exploit methods.\n\nconfig SLAB_BUCKETS\n\tbool \"Support allocation from separate kmalloc buckets\"\n\tdepends on !SLUB_TINY\n\tdefault SLAB_FREELIST_HARDENED\n\thelp\n\t  Kernel heap attacks frequently depend on being able to create\n\t  specifically-sized allocations with user-controlled contents\n\t  that will be allocated into the same kmalloc bucket as a\n\t  target object. To avoid sharing these allocation buckets,\n\t  provide an explicitly separated set of buckets to be used for\n\t  user-controlled allocations. This may very slightly increase\n\t  memory fragmentation, though in practice it's only a handful\n\t  of extra pages since the bulk of user-controlled allocations\n\t  are relatively long-lived.\n\n\t  If unsure, say Y.\n\nconfig SLUB_STATS\n\tdefault n\n\tbool \"Enable performance statistics\"\n\tdepends on SYSFS && !SLUB_TINY\n\thelp\n\t  The statistics are useful to debug slab allocation behavior in\n\t  order find ways to optimize the allocator. This should never be\n\t  enabled for production use since keeping statistics slows down\n\t  the allocator by a few percentage points. The slabinfo command\n\t  supports the determination of the most active slabs to figure\n\t  out which slabs are relevant to a particular load.\n\t  Try running: slabinfo -DA\n\nconfig RANDOM_KMALLOC_CACHES\n\tdefault n\n\tdepends on !SLUB_TINY\n\tbool \"Randomize slab caches for normal kmalloc\"\n\thelp\n\t  A hardening feature that creates multiple copies of slab caches for\n\t  normal kmalloc allocation and makes kmalloc randomly pick one based\n\t  on code address, which makes the attackers more difficult to spray\n\t  vulnerable memory objects on the heap for the purpose of exploiting\n\t  memory vulnerabilities.\n\n\t  Currently the number of copies is set to 16, a reasonably large value\n\t  that effectively diverges the memory objects allocated for different\n\t  subsystems or modules into different caches, at the expense of a\n\t  limited degree of memory and CPU overhead that relates to hardware and\n\t  system workload.\n\nendmenu # Slab allocator options\n\nconfig SHUFFLE_PAGE_ALLOCATOR\n\tbool \"Page allocator randomization\"\n\tdefault SLAB_FREELIST_RANDOM && ACPI_NUMA\n\thelp\n\t  Randomization of the page allocator improves the average\n\t  utilization of a direct-mapped memory-side-cache. See section\n\t  5.2.27 Heterogeneous Memory Attribute Table (HMAT) in the ACPI\n\t  6.2a specification for an example of how a platform advertises\n\t  the presence of a memory-side-cache. Okay, let's break this down. My initial observation is that all those `#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION` checks are correct in the context of `migrate_folio()` and similar page migration hooks. They're related to page migration in general, which can be triggered by things like COMPACTION or CMA, so keeping them conditional on `CONFIG_MIGRATION` is the right approach. They shouldn't be limited to `NUMA_MIGRATION` specifically.

Now, that `mm/gup.c` line 2198 and `FOLL_FORCE`... This refers to the `migrate_pages` logic within GUP. I need to make sure this is tied together correctly. If `gup.c` calls `migrate_pages()`, then if `migrate_pages()` is compiled (because `CONFIG_MIGRATION=y`), the long-term pinning code in GUP should also be compiled. So, that `#ifdef CONFIG_MIGRATION` is valid.

I need to confirm there is nothing amiss from the perspective of how NUMA's settings are handled in `mm/Kconfig`. Yes, `NUMA_MIGRATION` is the new config for NUMA page migration. Let's see how `NUMA_BALANCING` interacts with this. In `init/Kconfig`, it depends on `SMP && NUMA_MIGRATION && !PREEMPT_RT`. Right, that makes sense.

Let's do a sanity check on the Kconfig logic. If `NUMA` is enabled and the `MMU` is enabled, `NUMA_MIGRATION` should default to being enabled, which is right. Furthermore, `NUMA_MIGRATION` `select MIGRATION`. This means `MIGRATION` will be set to `y`.

The next point I need to verify is that making `MIGRATION` invisible to the user has not introduced any breaking changes. If a user tries to disable `MIGRATION` directly, they can't anymore because it's a `bool` without a prompt. They *can* disable `NUMA_MIGRATION`, which will make `MIGRATION` `n` unless something else selects it (like `COMPACTION`, `CMA`, `MEMORY_HOTREMOVE`, etc.). This seems like a potential change that needs deeper analysis.

Hmm, `MIGRATION` used to be user visible. Looking at the old Kconfig, if `COMPACTION` (or `CMA`, or `MEMORY_HOTREMOVE`) was enabled, users already could *not* turn off `MIGRATION`. The old configuration read:

```kconfig
config MIGRATION
	bool "Page migration"
	default y
	depends on (NUMA || MEMORY_HOTREMOVE || COMPACTION || CMA) && MMU
```
Since `COMPACTION` selected `MIGRATION`, the user ALREADY couldn't turn off `MIGRATION` if `COMPACTION` was enabled! (Kconfig `select` forces the target to `y`).

Since making `MIGRATION` invisible, the user CANNOT explicitly turn off `MIGRATION` if `COMPACTION` is enabled, because `COMPACTION` selects `MIGRATION`.

So `MIGRATION` being user-visible was mostly an illusion if `COMPACTION` (or `CMA`, or `MEMORY_HOTREMOVE`) was enabled.
The only case where `MIGRATION` was freely togglable by the user was when `NUMA` was `y` and `COMPACTION`/`CMA`/`MEMORY_HOTREMOVE` were all `n`.
In that exact case, the user could turn off `MIGRATION`.
Now, in that exact case, the user can turn off `NUMA_MIGRATION`, which will turn off `MIGRATION` (since nothing else selects it).
So the functionality for the user is exactly the same!
Instead of turning off "Page migration", they turn off "NUMA page migration".
This is conceptually cleaner and practically identical.

I'm starting to think this is a good refactor.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `move_pages` System Call and Configuration Dependencies**

Okay, so I started by confirming that if `CONFIG_SYSCALL_MOVE_PAGES` isn't enabled, the `move_pages` system call will correctly return `-ENOSYS` from `kernel/sys_ni.c`. That's the baseline.

My next step was to scrutinize the dependencies, specifically focusing on `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING`. I checked `init/Kconfig` and confirmed that the dependency tree for `NUMA_BALANCING` (which includes `ARCH_SUPPORTS_NUMA_BALANCING`, `!ARCH_WANT_NUMA_VARIABLE_LOCALITY`, `SMP`, `NUMA_MIGRATION`, and `!PREEMPT_RT`) is being checked correctly. This seemed to be in order.

I then dug into `mm/migrate.c`, looking at the code related to `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`. I noted that the `move_pages` system call and its supporting functions are indeed guarded by `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`. The previous code structure used `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA`, which I thought, was a bit confusing and would cause `move_pages` and its associated helpers to be compiled in some way with the old structure.

However, I started to question the placement of the `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` block, especially given its dependency on `NUMA_MIGRATION`. The critical point was whether `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` was dependent on `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` or not. This led me back to `init/Kconfig` and confirming that `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` *does* depend on `NUMA_MIGRATION`, which means when `NUMA_BALANCING` is true, `NUMA_MIGRATION` is necessarily true.

I re-examined the code diff from `mm/migrate.c`, where the crucial change was moving the `#endif /* CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION */` up. This placed the `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` block *outside* of the `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` block. I initially thought this might change whether it's compiled, but the dependency relationship made it safe. Since `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` depends on `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`, it will only be enabled when `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is enabled anyway. The change doesn't really matter. It's compiled anyway if `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` is true.

The commit message itself clarified the rationale: the original `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA` could be removed because both `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` and `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` depend on it.

So, the key realization is this: even though the code structure changed, the compiler behavior remains the same due to the dependency relationships. If `CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING` is true, `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` must also be true. The original `#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA` was redundant, and now the code compiles fine with the new structure.

To be extra thorough, I need to ensure `mm/migrate.c` is actually compiled when migration is enabled, which it is. I also need to check whether `CONFIG_MIGRATION` is enabled as the base, because `MIGRATION` enables a lot of functionality that this code depends upon. I need to make sure I don't see any other issues in `mm/mempolicy.c` and related files that depend upon `NUMA` and/or `MIGRATION` functionality.

It would be better if bind would truly restrict\n *                the allocation to memory nodes instead\n *\n * preferred      Try a specific node first before normal fallback.\n *                As a special case NUMA_NO_NODE here means do the allocation\n *                on the local CPU. This is\n *                similar to preferred without the special case.\n *\n * default        Allocate on the local node first, or when on a VMA\n *                use the process policy. Okay, so I'm looking at this `mbind` code, and it seems like if `migrate_folio_add` returns `false`, which it will do if `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` is disabled, then the `nr_failed` count will be incremented. Consequently, `queue_pages_range` will register a failure. Now, in `mbind`, if `nr_failed` is positive, it goes into a loop for binding the memory. When page migration isn't supported, the pages themselves won't move (hence the `nr_failed` increment), but the memory policy *will* be updated. If the `MPOL_MF_STRICT` flag is set, then the `err` variable gets set to `-EIO`, but that's what we want – it forces an error, because the strict policy demands the migration succeeds. This all aligns with the expected behavior of failing when attempting page migration and the pages can't move. Seems correct so far.

Now I'm diving into the `include/linux/memory-tiers.h` changes. Here, it uses `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION` instead of `CONFIG_MIGRATION` to determine whether we get the functions to do memory tiering demotion. That also makes perfect sense, as demotion means moving pages, i.e., migration.

And the `mm/memory-tiers.c` file follows the same pattern, gating demotion target and sysfs functionality on `CONFIG_NUMA_MIGRATION`.

Let's review `CONFIG_MIGRATION` in the Kconfig, and I notice it has changed. It's now an internal config selected by several other things – NUMA migration, hot removal, compaction, CMA, etc. It seems to be the "enabler" for the core migration code.
